priorities: Supply‑chain transparency and verification (TNFD, indigenous rights)
High‑impact, measurable environmental outcomes over carbon offsets
redLines: Any investment in companies with opaque or non‑transparent supply chains
Exposure to palm‑oil linked deforestation or lack of TNFD disclosure
Green‑washed ESG claims without verified impact
Investments that do not actively protect or restore natural ecosystems
preferredSectors: Companies with verified supply‑chain transparency and indigenous‑rights policies
avoidedSectors: Palm‑oil producers and downstream consumer goods reliant on deforestation
Companies with opaque commodity supply chains
Traditional fossil‑fuel or high‑emission sectors lacking nature‑risk disclosures
esgFocus: Indigenous land rights and long‑term monitoring

investmentBehavior.timeHorizon: long‑term, patient capital focused on multi‑year restoration outcomes
investmentBehavior.liquidity: low; prefers to sweep dividends and cash into foundation accounts rather than hold idle cash
investmentBehavior.mandate: Defensive Global Strategy with strict positive‑screening for biodiversity pioneers

lifeEvents.description: Launch of the private foundation's reforestation phase in Peru.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Diverted a portion of Q2 dividend streams to the NGO's field accounts.

lifeEvents.description: Capital call of USD 250,000 for a land‑acquisition project in the Atlantic Forest.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Trimmed low‑scoring ESG fixed‑income positions to fund the acquisition while keeping core sustainable equity intact.

lifeEvents.description: Elena’s field trip to the Amazon basin, reviewing on‑ground restoration projects.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Reinforced preference for companies investing in measurable rainforest restoration and indigenous protection.

lifeEvents.description: Set up a sub‑account for the foundation's youth educational programme.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Allocated cash reserves to support the educational initiative, aligning wealth with the next generation’s ecological education.

lifeEvents.description: Account handover protocol initiated due to Thomas Keller’s early retirement.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Ensured continuity of the highly customized sustainable investment mandate.

lifeEvents.description: Introductory call with new Relationship Manager Sarah Meier.
lifeEvents.portfolioImpact: Explicit expectation that the new RM maintain the same strict biodiversity and transparency standards.

interests: Field visits to conservation sites (Amazon, Borneo, Atlantic Forest)
quirks: Frequently doubles contributions to verified impact funds (e.g., Atlantic Forest Fund)
Uses Twitter hashtags to call out supply‑chain opacity
Insists on TNFD disclosure as a baseline for ESG credibility
rapportTriggers: Discussion of TNFD and nature‑related financial disclosures
Updates on Atlantic Forest Fund or similar biodiversity‑aligned projects
Mention of indigenous land‑rights and long‑term forest monitoring
Shared enthusiasm for Swiss Sustainable Finance events

keyQuotes.quote: We want to actively penalize companies that treat nature as a free resource. Our portfolio must prioritize businesses that proactively protect natural ecosystems.
Supply chain opaqueness is a redline for us, not a yellow flag.
Reforestation ≠ tree planting. Native species, indigenous land rights, 50-year monitoring — that's what Atlantic Forest Fund does. Everything else is marketing.
If a company takes a brave, absolute stance against palm oil deforestation and actively puts money into reforestation, that is exactly where my money should be.
The market is finally pricing nature risk, 10 years late. The investors who ignored this will spend the next decade catching up.
